name: notable curry
shortDescription: Notable curry is a fragrant blend with enough warmth, color, and complexity to make plain food seem suddenly well-traveled. Every seller claims a secret ratio, and every cook claims to know when someone has used too little.
flavorText: “Curry is not one spice speaking loudly; it is a crowded market agreeing on dinner.”

name: scarce all spice
shortDescription: Scarce all spice carries the curious trick of tasting like several winter cupboards at once: clove, pepper, nutmeg, and something harder to name. It is treasured by bakers, sausage makers, and traders who enjoy selling mystery by the spoonful.
flavorText: “All spice is what happens when a kitchen refuses to choose.”

name: scarce camphor
shortDescription: Scarce camphor has a sharp, cold scent that clears the head before the hand has even opened the packet. Apothecaries treat it carefully, perfumers respectfully, and insects with immediate regret.
flavorText: “Camphor smells like winter learned medicine.”

name: scarce cardamom
shortDescription: Scarce cardamom hides a bright, sweet perfume inside small green pods, as if a rich dessert were folded into a seed. A few pods can lift coffee, bread, wine, or courtship from ordinary to remembered.
flavorText: “Cardamom is expensive because it makes restraint taste generous.”

name: scarce cumin
shortDescription: Scarce cumin has a dry, earthy warmth that clings to stews, flatbreads, and roasted meats with stubborn confidence. It is not a pretty spice, but it makes food taste as though someone knew what they were doing.
flavorText: “Cumin is the road dust of good cooking.”

name: scarce lavender
shortDescription: Scarce lavender is a small purple luxury, useful in linen chests, bath oils, sleep draughts, and delicate sweets for people who enjoy confusing guests. Its scent calms rooms faster than apologies usually do.
flavorText: “Lavender is how a household pretends worry has left.”

name: scarce turmeric
shortDescription: Scarce turmeric stains fingers golden and turns food, cloth, and remedies toward the color of sun-warmed earth. Cooks value its bitterness and hue; healers value it for reasons they explain with suspicious certainty.
flavorText: “Turmeric leaves proof on every hand that touched it.”

name: fabled saffron
shortDescription: Fabled saffron is a treasure of red-gold threads, so light a pouch feels like a joke until the price is named. It colors rice, perfume, medicine, and pride with the same imperial confidence.
flavorText: “Saffron is harvested by patience and sold by audacity.”

name: earthy aromatic
shortDescription: An earthy aromatic blend, smelling of roots, damp bark, old cellars, and rain-soaked paths. It is used by perfumers who prefer depth over sweetness and by charm-workers who want a room to feel older than it is.
flavorText: “Earthy scents do not invite memory; they dig it up.”

name: musky aromatic
shortDescription: A musky aromatic with a warm animal depth that lingers on cloth long after the wearer has left. Some buyers call it seductive, some call it improper, and both groups return when the vial is nearly empty.
flavorText: “Musk is perfume that refuses to behave politely.”

name: sweet aromatic
shortDescription: A sweet aromatic, rounded with honeyed notes, soft resins, and the sort of fragrance that makes expensive rooms seem kinder. It is popular for gifts, festival oils, and letters that arrive carrying more meaning than ink.
flavorText: “Sweet scent is the cheapest way to make a memory lie nicely.”

name: vibrant aromatic
shortDescription: A vibrant aromatic that opens bright and lively, all citrus spark, sharp petals, and clean little surprises. It is worn by people who do not want to fade into a room and sold by merchants who know confidence has a smell.
flavorText: “Some perfumes whisper. This one enters laughing.”

name: fabled blue tears poison
shortDescription: Fabled blue tears poison gleams with a sorrowful beauty that makes the vial look like jewelry until the label is read twice. Assassins speak of it in rumors, physicians in warnings, and collectors with deeply questionable admiration.
flavorText: “The prettiest poisons always ask to be mistaken for mercy.”

name: nightshade poison
shortDescription: Nightshade poison is dark, bitter, and old enough in reputation that even the bottle seems guilty. It is the sort of substance no honest merchant displays openly unless honesty has become very profitable.
flavorText: “Nightshade does not need a story. Everyone has already heard one.”

name: scarce paralysis poison
shortDescription: Scarce paralysis poison is valued not for killing quickly, but for the more frightening art of stopping a body while leaving fear awake. Its buyers are watched carefully, and its sellers pretend that is enough.
flavorText: “A still enemy is not always a dead one, which is sometimes worse.”

name: frenzy poison
shortDescription: Frenzy poison is a cruel concoction meant to turn judgment into sparks and sparks into disaster. Battlefield rumors, tavern tales, and locked alchemy notes all agree on one thing: nobody nearby stays merely a witness for long.
flavorText: “Frenzy poison does not choose sides; it simply opens the cage.”

name: weakness poison
shortDescription: Weakness poison steals strength without the drama of blood, leaving hands unreliable and knees suddenly honest. Cowards prize it, tacticians respect it, and duelists fear it more than any theatrical blade coating.
flavorText: “The cruelest poisons do not kill the body; they disappoint it.”

name: notable fire resistance potion
shortDescription: A notable fire resistance potion, warm-colored and faintly smoky, brewed for kitchens, forges, dragon roads, and overconfident heroes. It does not make flame friendly, but it may convince flame to argue longer before winning.
flavorText: “Fire can be bargained with, but never trusted.”

name: fabled brilliance potion
shortDescription: A fabled brilliance potion said to clear thought, sharpen wit, and make the world briefly arrange itself into useful patterns. Scholars desire it, gamblers misuse it, and tired merchants dream of drinking one before tax season.
flavorText: “Brilliance in a bottle is still dangerous if opened by a fool.”

name: notable agility potion
shortDescription: A notable agility potion with a lively shimmer, brewed for quick feet, quicker hands, and escapes that need to look graceful afterward. Dancers, thieves, duelists, and roof repairers all claim legitimate reasons to own one.
flavorText: “Speed is cheaper than courage and often mistaken for it.”

name: market sleep potion
shortDescription: A market sleep potion, mild by alchemical standards and still treated with respect by anyone who has ever overslept a ferry. It smells of bitter herbs under honey, the classic scent of medicine trying to seem kind.
flavorText: “Sleep bought in a bottle always comes with questions in the morning.”

name: scarce stealth potion
shortDescription: A scarce stealth potion with a muted sheen, brewed for quiet soles, softened shadows, and the profitable art of not being noticed. Honest uses exist, though sellers hear them less often than buyers claim.
flavorText: “A stealth potion is most convincing when no one admits it was sold.”

name: rough digestive potion
shortDescription: A rough digestive potion, cloudy, bitter, and sold with the confidence of someone who has seen worse meals than yours. It is not pleasant, but neither is regretting a market pie for three days.
flavorText: “Digestive potions exist because cooks are optimistic and stomachs keep records.”

name: scarce wakefulness potion
shortDescription: A scarce wakefulness potion that smells sharp enough to insult sleep directly. Night watchmen, desperate scholars, caravan guards, and new parents all respect it, though only some admit how much they need it.
flavorText: “Borrowed alertness always collects interest.”

name: market healing potion
shortDescription: A market healing potion, practical, familiar, and trusted for cuts, bruises, and the ordinary injuries of an unreasonable world. It is not miraculous, but a shelf without one feels like an invitation to regret.
flavorText: “Healing potions are bought before bravery and after stupidity.”

name: market strength potion
shortDescription: A market strength potion with a dense color and the faint smell of iron, brewed for heavy doors, harder blows, and workers pretending they are not tired. The best merchants sell it with a warning about confidence, though few buyers listen.
flavorText: “Strength in a bottle still needs a sensible hand.”

name: fabled elixir of life
shortDescription: A fabled elixir of life, spoken of in trembling voices by the sick, the rich, and the terrified powerful. Whether it restores years or merely hope, even a rumor of such a bottle can make kingdoms behave foolishly.
flavorText: “Immortality is the only luxury that frightens its own buyer.”

name: scarce courage potion
shortDescription: A scarce courage potion brewed for shaking hands, locked knees, and moments when fear has become inconvenient. True courage is still required afterward, but the first swallow may help a person remember where they left it.
flavorText: “Courage potion does not remove fear; it lends fear a shorter leash.”

name: notable calming potion
shortDescription: A notable calming potion with a pale, steady color and the smell of clean linen after rain. It is used by healers, negotiators, grieving households, and anyone expected to speak gently while furious.
flavorText: “Calm is cheaper in bottles than in apologies, but not always stronger.”

name: notable love potion
shortDescription: A notable love potion, sweet-smelling and ethically troublesome, carried in shops that keep their curtains half drawn. Some buy it for romance, some for vanity, and the wisest leave it sealed as a reminder of what desire should not purchase.
flavorText: “Love that needs a cork is usually something else.”

name: rough unknown potion
shortDescription: A rough unknown potion of uncertain color, uncertain smell, and absolutely certain risk. It may be a bargain, a mistake, or the start of a story told by people standing a safe distance away.
flavorText: “Unknown potions are proof that curiosity has poor survival instincts.”

name: scarce vision potion
shortDescription: A scarce vision potion brewed to sharpen sight, reveal hidden marks, or trouble the mind with things it was not ready to notice. Scouts prize it, seers argue over it, and cowards fear what clarity might cost.
flavorText: “Better vision is not always better news.”

name: market hearing potion
shortDescription: A market hearing potion meant to sharpen the ear for whispers, hoofbeats, loose floorboards, and conversations that were not offered freely. It is legal in some towns, suspicious in most, and useful everywhere.
flavorText: “The problem with hearing more is learning how much was said softly.”

name: market balance potion
shortDescription: A market balance potion used by sailors, acrobats, roofers, and anyone whose dignity depends on remaining upright. It steadies the body for a while, though it cannot help much with poor judgment.
flavorText: “Balance in the feet is easier than balance in the life.”

name: market stamina potion
shortDescription: A market stamina potion with a hearty bitterness, sold to couriers, dockhands, long-road guards, and merchants with inventories longer than their patience. It keeps the body moving after common sense has suggested chairs.
flavorText: “Stamina potions are what people buy when rest feels too expensive.”

name: expired moldy potion
shortDescription: An expired moldy potion whose surface, smell, and moral character have all declined. It might still do something, but anyone eager to discover what should be charged extra for the cleanup.
flavorText: “Some potions age into wine. This one aged into a warning.”

name: burn remedy
shortDescription: Burn remedy is a cooling salve or draught kept near ovens, forges, campfires, and kitchens with more confidence than skill. It smells of herbs, oil, and the quiet gratitude of skin no longer shouting.
flavorText: “Keep burn remedy near any place where people say, ‘It is not that hot.’”

name: hangover remedy
shortDescription: Hangover remedy is a bitter mercy for mornings after songs, wagers, poor decisions, and ale stronger than memory. It cannot restore dignity, but it may help the sufferer sit upright while searching for it.
flavorText: “No remedy cures the story of why you needed it.”

name: illness remedy
shortDescription: Illness remedy is a household staple against fever, cough, chills, and all the unnamed miseries that make work impossible. Families keep it on high shelves, not because it is rare, but because hope should not be misplaced.
flavorText: “A good remedy does not promise miracles; it promises another morning.”

name: mental remedy
shortDescription: Mental remedy is brewed for fogged thoughts, frayed nerves, and the peculiar exhaustion that sleep does not fix. It is handled gently by decent healers, because troubled minds deserve more than a strong bottle and a quick sale.
flavorText: “The mind is not a lock, but some remedies know where to knock.”

name: soreness remedy
shortDescription: Soreness remedy is a practical comfort for aching shoulders, bruised backs, long marches, and honest labor that went on too long. It smells like menthol, roots, and the quiet promise that tomorrow might be less rude.
flavorText: “Soreness is the body’s invoice; this helps negotiate payment.”

name: neutral solution
shortDescription: Neutral solution is a plain alchemical base, valued precisely because it begins without strong opinions. It dilutes, carries, rinses, and prepares other mixtures, doing the humble work that lets dramatic bottles take the credit.
flavorText: “Neutral solution is the blank page of the alchemist’s shelf.”

name: acid solution
shortDescription: Acid solution is a biting, glass-stoppered liquid used for etching, cleaning, dissolving, and regrettable demonstrations. A careful hand can make good use of it; an impatient one can ruin tools, tables, and friendships.
flavorText: “Acid has no temper. That is what makes it frightening.”

name: water solution
shortDescription: Water solution is the simplest-looking bottle on the shelf, though alchemists insist simplicity is not the same as uselessness. Clean, measured, and ready to carry other ingredients, it is where many careful mixtures begin.
flavorText: “Even magic needs something to dissolve into.”

name: moss solution
shortDescription: Moss solution is green, damp-smelling, and faintly forested, used in salves, growth tonics, and mixtures that prefer shade to sunlight. It has the look of a swamp bottled by someone with tidy handwriting.
flavorText: “Moss does not hurry, but it reaches everything eventually.”

name: bacterial solution
shortDescription: Bacterial solution is a troubling alchemical culture, useful to skilled researchers and dangerous in nearly every careless context. The bottle may look quiet, but good practitioners know quiet life can multiply faster than blame.
flavorText: “Never trust a solution simply because it has stopped bubbling.”

name: fungus solution
shortDescription: Fungus solution carries the earthy bite of spores, rot, damp wood, and useful decay. It is prized for medicines, poisons, inks, and experiments that begin with careful notes and end with sealed windows.
flavorText: “Fungus teaches that ruin is sometimes only preparation.”

name: cauldron
shortDescription: A soot-dark cauldron used by cooks, healers, and less honest alchemists alike. Its rim is worn smooth from years of stirring, and the inside carries the ghost of bitter herbs, boiled roots, and experiments that should probably not be named.
flavorText: “A good cauldron remembers every recipe. A dangerous one remembers every mistake.”

name: defuser
shortDescription: A defuser is an alchemical tool for calming volatile mixtures, bleeding off pressure, or convincing a bad idea to become slightly less immediate. It is most valued by people who have already learned the sound of glass failing.
flavorText: “A defuser is what an alchemist buys after the first ceiling stain.”

name: influser
shortDescription: An influser pushes essence, vapor, or reagent into a waiting base, giving subtle ingredients a way to become impossible to ignore. Apothecaries use it for delicate work; amateurs use it to make mistakes more efficiently.
flavorText: “Infusion is persuasion, provided the liquid is willing.”
